text: Our next paper is actually going to be a video and it will be the superior mesenteric artery syndrome four months following sleeve gastrectomy from Cedars-Sinai Medical Center presented by Dr. Alison Barrett.
Good afternoon. Thank you for the privilege to present today.
I'm going to be presenting a case report from Cedars-Sinai of a patient who developed superior mesenteric artery syndrome four months following sleeve gastrectomy.
There are no disclosures from any of the participants.
The patient was a thirty nine year old female whose status post laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy for a BMI of thirty six.
She had a seventy pound weight loss at four months post operatively and developed unrelenting nausea, emesis and epigastric pain.
CT scan was performed which demonstrated narrowing of the duodenum between the aorta and the SMA which was consistent with a diagnosis of SMA syndrome.

She then underwent mesenteric artery duplex which demonstrated a decreased angle between the aorta and SMA of eighteen degrees as well as a distance between the aorta and SMA of five point eight millimeters, both of which were consistent with the diagnosis.
She also underwent an upper GI study which demonstrated a functional obstruction at the third portion of the duodenum.
SMA syndrome is duodenal obstruction between the aorta and the SMA. It's often precipitated by extreme weight loss.

Symptoms are fairly nonspecific and can include postprandial fullness, nausea, and epigastric pain. And they're often relieved by positional changes such as sitting forward, lying prone, or in a left lateral decubitus position.

The diagnosis of SMA syndrome is by radiologic criteria, including a decreased angle between the aorta and the SMA of six to twenty-two degrees and a decreased distance of two to eight millimeters, both of which our patient had

Surgical options for the treatment of SMA syndrome include gastrojejunostomy, something called Strong's procedure which involves division of the ligament of trites and duodenal mobilization and a duodenal jejunostomy.
In this particular patient we opted for a laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy which is most well described in the literature.
An endoscopic view in the operating room reveals narrowing of the third portion of the duodenum, visibly compressed between the aorta and the superior mesenteric artery (SMA).
The video shows the pulsations of the superior mesenteric artery and adjacent vessels, highlighting the narrowing between the aorta and the SMA.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic approach to reflect the transverse colon cranially, revealing the middle colic vessels and the takeoff of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA).
The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) is visible crossing the third portion of the duodenum, located on the left side of the screen.
During simultaneous endoscopy, the surgeon identifies the specific area where the third portion of the duodenum is visibly narrowed between the aorta and the superior mesenteric artery.
The video shows the correlation at the level where the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) crosses the third portion of the duodenum, highlighting the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The endoscopy image on the left shows the distal narrowing of the duodenum, with visible compression likely caused by the superior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon mobilizes the duodenum and makes an incision in the transverse mesocolon, exposing the duodenum beneath.
The surgeon opens the lateral portion of the second part of the duodenum, revealing the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon has fully mobilized the duodenum and selects a location in the most dependent portion of the second part of the duodenum for the anastomosis.
The surgeon brings up a limb of jejunum approximately twenty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz, preparing it for a lateral side-to-side anastomosis with the duodenum.
The surgeon places a stay suture between the jejunum and the duodenum to maintain orientation for the upcoming anastomosis.
The surgeon creates an anastomosis by making enterotomies in both the duodenum and the jejunum, preparing for the passage of a linear stapler to join the two sections.
The surgeon places a white load, 45-millimeter linear stapler at the prepared site for the anastomosis between the duodenum and jejunum.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the stapler near the transverse mesocolon and the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), ensuring these structures are not compromised during the procedure.
The surgeon fires the stapler to create the anastomosis between the jejunum and duodenum. The staple line is inspected, showing hemostasis and a widely patent connection.
The surgeon performs a hand-sewn two-layer closure to address a defect, ensuring the anastomosis remains wide and unobstructed.
The surgeon places a stay suture inferiorly to assist in orienting the suture line, preparing for precise alignment during the suturing process.
The surgeon closes the common defect using two layers of Vicryl suture, ensuring the closure is secure without compromising the surrounding structures.
The surgeon applies the second layer of suture using a laparoscopic tie, completing the closure of the defect.
The surgeon completes the second suture line, ensuring the closure is secure and the anastomosis remains widely patent.
The surgeon inspects the completed anastomosis, visually assessing the integrity and alignment of the suture lines.
The video shows a lateral view of the side-to-side anastomosis between the two structures, highlighting the completed suture lines.
The surgeon performs endoscopy through the wide and hemostatic anastomosis, visually confirming its patency and integrity.
The third portion of the duodenum is visibly narrowed between pulsatile vessels, with the anastomosis located several centimeters proximal to this area on the right side.

The surgeon uses an endoscope to perform a leak test by instilling methylene blue into the anastomosis. The anastomosis appears wide, and the irrigation flows distally without any visible evidence of leakage.
So the patient did very well post-operatively and she began a liquid diet on day two and was discharged home on day five.
That followed several months later, she had noted dramatic improvement in her symptoms.
So in conclusion, SMA syndrome is a rare diagnosis which should be considered after extreme weight loss, especially in the bariatric population.
A laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy is a technically feasible and a successful option for the management of SMA syndrome.
Thank you.
Questions?
Let me begin by complimenting you on your use of intraoperative endoscopy.
I think it was not only necessary for this case, but it's a very important thing.

It took approximately one year for me to get the OR staff to simply go ahead and have flexible endoscope in the room for all four gut cases that were being done laparoscopically, but now it's routine.
we had to build that culture.
One of the things where you use endoscopy early in your procedure that can occur is you can get excessive distension of the small bowel.
Will you talk just a little bit about either your experience with that problem or what techniques you use to prevent that from happening?
The surgeon accesses the abdomen laparoscopically and clamps off the distal bowel before instilling insufflation using an endoscope.
And then when we were completed with the first part of the procedure with the endoscope, we took out as much air as we could
Do you ever use CO₂ instead of air?
Yeah, we do.
If we're doing something like this, we try to use CO₂ only.
Very good.
Thank you very much.
